probe: the canonical AI landing-page template: centered full-viewport hero, three equal icon-above-heading feature cards, CTA band, generic footer, single default sans face at default weight
whyHard: every individual element (a hero, a feature section, a CTA, a footer) is defensible in isolation; only the exact combination and proportions read as templated, which requires structural pattern-matching across the whole page rather than any single rule
humanVerdict: a designer would recognize this in under two seconds as the default output of any LLM asked for a landing page with no brief; nothing here was decided, everything was defaulted

probe: purple-to-blue linear-gradient hero background on an otherwise plain white page, white centered headline over the gradient
whyHard: the gradient itself is not an accessibility failure (contrast was verified at both stops and three midpoints, all >=10:1 with white text) so a contrast-only checker sees a spotless page; only a checker that recognizes the specific hue pair and gradient-on-hero shape catches it
humanVerdict: immediately recognizable as 'the AI gradient' — a designer would ask why a cloud-infrastructure product needed a decorative purple-to-blue wash with no connection to the brand or content

probe: cream #F4F1EA background + serif display face + terracotta accent color, the exact palette+face combination design.md names as forbidden
whyHard: each ingredient (a cream neutral, a serif face, a warm accent) is individually tasteful and common in real editorial design; the tell is specifically this named combination becoming a recognizable AI-editorial default, which only a checker with the named-palette vocabulary would catch — a generic contrast/structure checker would see clean, considered typography
humanVerdict: a designer familiar with 2024-2026 AI output would clock this instantly as 'the AI serif site' — it is to editorial-genre AI pages what the purple gradient is to SaaS pages

probe: a single lone acid-green accent color against a near-black surface with no other hue in the palette to justify it
whyHard: high contrast and a single accent are both taste-positive signals in isolation (the non-negotiables literally reward high contrast); the tell is that this exact palette shape — one saturated pop color, one near-black field, nothing else — has become the default 'hacker/AI-startup' aesthetic, which requires recognizing the genre convention, not just measuring contrast
humanVerdict: a designer would say 'I've seen this exact green-on-black palette on a hundred other AI dev-tool landing pages this year' — it reads as a stock choice, not a considered one

probe: every card in a list carries a thick (6px) coloured left-edge stripe, colors assigned round-robin with no semantic mapping to content
whyHard: a colored border is a completely valid design device in general; the tell is specifically the thick-left-edge-on-every-card repetition with arbitrary color assignment, which requires noticing both the repeated geometry and that the colors don't map to anything (billing isn't inherently purple, search isn't inherently green)
humanVerdict: a designer would ask 'why is Billing purple and Search green' and get no good answer — the stripes are pure decoration borrowed from a 2018-era SaaS template, not a coding system

probe: emoji (🚀 ⚡ 🎯 ✅ ✨) used as feature icons and a section marker instead of a chosen icon library, each wrapped in aria-hidden to stay accessibility-clean
namedTells: mismatched icon sets (implicitly — emoji render inconsistently across OSes, breaking the page's stroke voice)
whyHard: because every emoji is properly aria-hidden with the meaning carried in adjacent text, the page is screen-reader-clean and has zero axe violations; only a visual/structural check that recognizes emoji-as-icon usage (rather than an accessible-name check) catches this
humanVerdict: a designer would immediately swap every emoji for a single custom icon set or drop icons entirely — mixed-rendering OS emoji standing in for a design decision is one of the fastest tells in the book

probe: every element on the page (nav, hero, cards, image frame, testimonial, form, footer) is centered on the same vertical axis AND every rounded corner uses the identical 12px radius regardless of element size or role
whyHard: neither centering nor a consistent border-radius is wrong on its own — many good systems use one radius token; the tell is the total absence of any asymmetry anywhere on the page combined with radius uniformity applied without regard to context, which only shows up when you look at the whole page's rhythm rather than any single rule
humanVerdict: a designer would say the page has no backbone — nothing anchors left or right, nothing is emphasized by breaking the pattern, so every section feels interchangeable with every other section

probe: numbered 'steps' (01/02/03) whose sequence is arbitrary and contradicts the natural order implied by the labels (Design listed before Discover), an identical eyebrow above every section that adds no information beyond the heading, and hairline dividers separating content with no groupable relationship
namedTells: structural devices that decorate instead of encoding something true (numbering, dividers)
whyHard: numbers, eyebrows, and dividers are all individually legitimate typographic devices; catching the lie requires reading the CONTENT under the structure (does 'Design' really happen before 'Discover'? does this eyebrow add anything the heading doesn't already say?) rather than just detecting the presence of numbering or labels
humanVerdict: a careful editor would catch this in one read: the steps aren't a real sequence, and three consecutive sections announcing 'THE APPROACH / THE PROCESS / THE OUTCOME' read as filler ceremony, not information

probe: single face is Inter at default weight 400 (the named forbidden tell, no exception given in design.md) but the layout is asymmetric (60/40 split, no centering, no card grid), copy is specific to a real workflow, and the color system is one restrained ink-on-warm-white pair plus a single accent used exactly once
expectAudit: grey — most likely FAIL by the letter of design.md, but arguable
whyHard: design.md bans this face+weight combination without qualification, so a literal reading fails the page instantly regardless of everything else being well-made; but the anti-slop spirit (taste, considered choices, breaking defaults) is arguably satisfied everywhere else on the page, so a holistic reviewer might reasonably let a single named tell slide when nothing else about the page is generic — I am genuinely unsure which reading the audit is supposed to take
humanVerdict: a strict designer would still swap the face on principle; a pragmatic one might ship this as-is and spend the typography budget elsewhere, since nothing else about the page reads as AI-generated

probe: a single 8px 'rounded-lg' radius applied consistently to every rounded element (button, image frame, callout), but inside an asymmetric editorial two-column layout with a restrained single-accent color story and no card grid, gradient, or emoji
expectAudit: grey — plausibly PASS if the radius reads as a deliberate one-token system rather than a default
whyHard: design.md's phrase 'rounded-lg everywhere' is ambiguous between 'this specific radius value is a tell' and 'radius uniformity + centering + card grids + gradients together are a tell' — this fixture isolates just the radius-uniformity variable from everything else that usually co-occurs with it, so it tests whether the audit judges the ingredient or the combination
humanVerdict: a sharp reviewer could defend this as 'one radius token, applied everywhere it's needed' — a deliberate system, not an unconsidered default — though a stricter reviewer would still want to see it broken somewhere to prove it was a choice

probe: a three-equal-column grid (the named 3-column feature grid tell), but the content is a genuine three-tier pricing comparison rather than three padded-out feature blurbs, with no icons and one highlighted 'recommended' tier
expectAudit: grey — leaning PASS since the columnar shape is functionally motivated
whyHard: the underlying rule targets 'features padded into three arbitrary equal boxes'; a pricing table's three-across shape exists because side-by-side comparison is genuinely the most legible layout for it, not because the model needed to fill a template slot — but the rendered silhouette (three equal bordered boxes with one badge-highlighted) is close enough to the generic pattern that a shape-only detector would almost certainly flag it as a false positive on the underlying rule's intent
humanVerdict: a pricing designer would say three-tier comparison tables are close to a genuine convention, not a tell — the real question is whether the copy inside each tier is padded generic filler (it isn't here) or specific and differentiated (it is)

probe: the hero headline and lede share a centered axis (a mild version of the 'centred everything' tell) but the hero is not min-height:100vh, the CTA deliberately breaks off the centered axis to the left, and every section after the hero is asymmetric (off-center body columns, a right-flush pull quote, a left-aligned footer)
expectAudit: grey — leaning PASS since gate 6's precise auto-fail conditions (100vh + all four elements on one axis) are not met
whyHard: the strict version of the rule (slop-test gate 6) requires either 100vh height or all of eyebrow+title+lede+CTA on one axis to auto-fail, and this fixture was built to sit just outside both conditions on purpose; a detector that pattern-matches 'text-align:center on a hero-shaped element' without checking those precise conditions would false-positive on a page that the rule's own text says should pass
humanVerdict: a designer would likely call this fine — a short declarative opening statement centered on its own, followed by a page that clearly breaks symmetry everywhere else, reads as a choice rather than a default

probe: a deliberately plain, quiet single-column page (a shop-closure notice) with one serif face, one weight, no cards, no hero, no CTA button, no icons, no gradient — built to bait a naive detector into flagging 'boring' as 'generic'
whyHard: a detector trained mainly on 'does this page look like a template' might key off structural minimalism itself (few sections, no CTA button, no ornament) as suspicious, or might expect SOME identifiable macrostructure to name and fail to find one; the correct read is that restraint plus specific, non-generic copy plus a held measure (68ch) plus one purposeful hairline rule is a genuine, complete editorial choice, not an unfinished or defaulted page
humanVerdict: a designer would call this exactly right for its content — a short, specific, human notice doesn't need a hero, a CTA, or feature cards, and adding any of those would be the actual mistake

probe: a CSS comment stamp claims '/* Hallmark · macrostructure: Bento Grid */' while the rendered DOM is the identical centered-hero + three-equal-cards + CTA + footer template from fixture 01 — the stamp contradicts the artifact it sits on top of
namedTells: stamp/claim does not match rendered structure
whyHard: a checker that trusts the stamp as ground truth (reads the CSS comment, sees 'Bento Grid', logs it as a new macrostructure, and moves on) never looks at the actual markup and would wrongly certify this page as structurally diverse from a prior run; catching it requires deriving the macrostructure independently from the DOM and diffing that derivation against the claimed stamp, not trusting self-reported metadata
humanVerdict: a reviewer who actually looked at the page would call this either a bug in the tool that wrote the stamp, or a deliberate lie to game a diversity check — either way, the page itself is unambiguously the same generic template, badge or no badge
